content:
This site is organized as a small technical art library. Use the top navigation to jump into VEX, Python, Rigging, Motion Graphic workflows.
How the mock content works # Each module page is a Hugo section page. The cards below each module come from normal Markdown article bundles with front matter for tags, categories, series, and series_order.
Inside articles, ## and ### headings automatically build the table of contents when showTableOfContents is enabled.
title: Learning Lab

content:
This mock project describes the current site organization.
What changed # Navigation # Top-level menu items now point to learning tracks and dropdowns.
Content model # Each module is a Hugo section with cards, tags, categories, and series data.
Homepage # The homepage now pulls recent entries from the projects section.
Next edits # Replace mock titles, dates, summaries, and images with your own production work.
title: Procedural Learning Hub

content: Project pages are what the homepage uses for the latest-project cards.

content:
Use this placeholder for bridging generated frames into production comps.
Handoff checklist # Separate layers # Recreate foreground, midground, background, and graphic overlays as editable pieces.
Match movement # Decide what should animate and what should stay as texture.
Version notes # Track which prompt, model, and cleanup pass created the source frame.
title: Prompt to Comp Handoff

content:
This page comes from content/videos/_index.md trying to make it bigger. # It represents the videos folder itself, so its URL is /videos/.
The file content/videos/example.md is a normal page inside this folder, so its URL is /videos/example/.
This sentence was added to test the full Hugo publish workflow.
title: Videos

content:
This article is a future template for AI-assisted styleframe development.
Workflow # Prompt direction # Write prompts around composition, material, lighting, and intent.
Selection pass # Choose frames for design usefulness, not novelty.
Cleanup pass # Rebuild the selected frame into a controlled layout.
title: AI Styleframe System

content:
This placeholder is for picker UI design notes.
Picker rules # Zones # Group controls by body area instead of construction hierarchy.
States # Show selected, mirrored, hidden, and locked controls clearly.
Hand-off # Keep labels short so animators can scan the picker quickly.
title: Animator Picker Layout

content: Rigging is split between Houdini and Maya so procedural experiments and animator-facing production rigs can each have a clear home.

content:
This mock article stores the eventual ribbon spine breakdown.
Build order # Guide placement # Define chest, waist, and hip guide points.
Surface setup # Create the ribbon surface and bind control joints.
Animator controls # Expose the few controls that matter and hide the construction system.

content:
This placeholder project shows how a second project appears on the homepage and Projects page.
Sprint goals # Small tools # Collect simple scripts that remove repeated clicks.
Documentation # Write short articles while each tool is fresh.
Review # Mark what should become a polished tool and what should stay as a note.
title: Houdini Tool Sprint

content:
This article will become a research page for facial rig structure.
Control layers # Primary shapes # Large expression controls for brows, lids, mouth corners, and jaw.
Secondary offsets # Small corrective controls for asymmetry and polish.
Export interface # Standardize channel names before sending data downstream.
title: Procedural Facial Rig Notes

content: Mock Houdini rigging modules for procedural character systems.
title: Rigging for Houdini

content:
This module is a placeholder for a biped rig prototype.
Skeleton pass # Joint layout # Start with a readable hierarchy and stable naming.
Control pass # Create simple controls that prove animator intent before adding detail.
Deformation pass # Test the minimum skinning setup needed for feedback.
Notes # Keep the first pass boring and easy to debug.
title: KineFX Biped Blockout

content: Python is split by DCC so each workflow can keep its own assumptions, APIs, and examples.
title: Python

content: Mock modules for Maya automation and artist-facing tools.
title: Python for Maya

content:
This module is a placeholder for a clean viewport review workflow.
Review data # Shot metadata # Capture frame range, camera, artist, and version number.
Overlay text # Render readable overlays for department, date, and scene path.
Delivery # Save a playblast to the expected review folder and open it after export.
title: Viewport Review Helper

content:
This article is a scaffold for a Maya publish tool.
Publish stages # Collect scene data # Gather asset name, version, department, and target output path.
Validate rig and geometry # Check namespaces, frozen transforms, unknown nodes, and missing references.
Export # Write the output and generate a short publish report.
Mock command # import maya.cmds as cmds selection = cmds.ls(selection=True)
title: Asset Publisher Mockup

content:
Use this as a future home for scripts that find bypassed nodes, missing labels, broken references, and heavy caches.
Audit targets # Naming # Check whether important nodes follow the expected prefix and suffix rules.
Dependencies # Report missing files, absolute local paths, and disabled cache nodes.
Performance # Flag expensive nodes before the publish step.
Output format # Start with a simple table: node path, severity, message, and suggested action.
title: Node Graph Audit

content: Mock modules for Houdini automation. Replace these with real notes as you build tools.
title: Python for Houdini

content:
This mock article is a place to collect repeatable Houdini shelf-tool patterns.
Tool shape # Inputs # Start with the selected nodes, current network editor, or active viewer state.
Validation # Give clear messages when artists select the wrong node type.
Output # Create nodes, add labels, and keep the network readable.
Example skeleton # import hou nodes = hou.selectedNodes() if not nodes: hou.ui.displayMessage("Select at least one node.")
title: Shelf Toolkit Starter

content:
This mock article stores a future typography system breakdown.
System parts # Grid # Define margins, title area, supporting text, and safe zones.
Type scale # Keep a small set of reusable text sizes.
Motion rules # Use timing patterns that can repeat across a package.
title: Title System Study

content: Mock modules for traditional motion graphic practice and production habits.
title: Traditional Workflow

content:
This module is a placeholder for constraint recipes that can be reused across motion, rigging, and destruction setups.
Constraint anatomy # Every constraint needs a stable name, a target pair, and a rule for how it should behave.
Outline # Build anchors # Create named points from source geometry and preserve the original ids.
Measure relationships # Use distance thresholds to decide which anchors should connect.
Export for simulation # Write the final attributes as a clean interface for the next node.
Mock snippet # float d = distance(@P, point(1, "P", @ptnum)); i@active = d < chf("max_distance");
title: Procedural Constraints in VEX

content: VEX is its own track because it is both a language and a way of thinking in Houdini. These mock modules are starter placeholders for wrangles, solvers, constraints, and procedural systems.
title: VEX

content:
This module is for visual language and board development.
Board structure # Primitive set # Choose the circles, rectangles, lines, and masks that define the system.
Contrast rules # Decide how scale, density, and color guide attention.
Animation hints # Mark which shapes should transform, reveal, or hold.
title: Shape Language Board

content:
This placeholder article shows the article-level structure: series block, tags, and an outline generated from Markdown headings.
Goal # Create predictable attributes that downstream nodes can read without extra cleanup.
Core ideas # Attribute classes # Use point attributes for per-point behavior, primitive attributes for per-face decisions, and detail attributes for shared settings.
Naming pattern # Mock convention:
f@fx_mask = clamp(@Cd.r, 0.0, 1.0); s@module = "hero_scatter"; Practice task # Build a small wrangle that creates fx_mask, variant_id, and module attributes from a test grid.
title: Attribute Wrangling Basics
